title: SEGMENT 11 — Lisa Pisano: pig kidney plus pig thymus, April 2024

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Lisa Pisano: pig kidney plus pig thymus, April 2024
The next patient was Lisa Pisano, a 54-year-old woman from New Jersey, transplanted on April 12, 2024 — just under a month after Slayman — at NYU Langone Health in New York City, by a team led by Dr.
Robert Montgomery.
Pisano had a particular combination of problems.
She had end-stage kidney disease, like Slayman, and she had been on dialysis.
But she also had a failing heart — she had received a mechanical pump called a left ventricular assist device to keep her circulation going.
Most patients with that severity of cardiac disease are not candidates for a kidney transplant, because the surgery itself is risky and a kidney transplant doesn't solve their cardiac problem.
She was, in transplant terms, a no-options patient.
Montgomery's team did something different with Pisano.
They didn't just transplant a pig kidney.
They transplanted a pig kidney with attached pig thymus tissue.
I want to spend a minute on why.
The thymus is the organ in your chest where your T cells finish their development.

When you're young, your T cells are made in the bone marrow and then migrate to the thymus, where they undergo a kind of finishing school — a process of selection in which T cells that react too strongly to "self" antigens are killed, and T cells that don't react at all are also killed.
The ones that survive — the ones that react just enough — are released into the bloodstream as mature T cells.
The thymus is, in effect, an education facility for T cells, where they learn to recognize self versus non-self.
Here is the idea behind the thymokidney approach.
If you transplant a pig kidney along with a piece of the donor pig's thymus, you are placing inside the human a small fragment of a foreign immune education facility.
Some of the human's developing T cells will pass through that foreign thymus and be educated by it.
They will learn to recognize the pig's tissue as "self.
" When they emerge as mature T cells, they will tolerate the pig kidney instead of attacking it.

This was a concept that had been worked out in earlier preclinical baboon experiments by a researcher named David Sachs, who spent decades on the idea of inducing donor-specific tolerance through thymic tissue.
The Pisano case was, in a sense, the first time the concept was tried in a human alongside a clinical kidney transplant.
Pisano's surgery itself went well.
The kidney took.
Her cardiac status was monitored closely.
She came off dialysis.
She had a functioning pig kidney and a functioning pig thymus inside her.
The clinical course, unfortunately, was difficult.
Pisano's underlying cardiovascular disease worsened in the weeks after the surgery.

The kidney itself was functioning — but her LVAD developed problems, her cardiac output declined, and she was returned to dialysis when blood pressure could not be maintained at adequate levels to perfuse the kidney.
The kidney was eventually removed.
Pisano herself died in July 2024, about three months after the original transplant, of cardiac causes that the team has reported were largely independent of the xenograft.
So Pisano's outcome was, from the immunological standpoint, also a partial success — the kidney functioned for weeks without rejection, the thymokidney approach was tolerated.

But from the clinical standpoint, the patient died, and the team learned that patient selection — choosing patients robust enough to survive the broader surgical and medical course — was going to be its own learning curve.
What Pisano's case added to the field's knowledge was: the kidney plus thymus combination is feasible.
The thymus tissue can be transplanted alongside the kidney without overwhelming complications.
Whether the thymus is actually inducing tolerance, in the long-term immunological sense, remains an open question — but the surgical concept has been demonstrated.
Two patients so far.
Two kidneys.
Both functioned.
Both patients eventually died, neither from rejection.
The third patient is, in some ways, the most interesting case of the three.
